19: Zerubbabel, son of Pedaiah (or Shealtiel in Haggai 1:1; Ezra 3:2 — a minor genealogical discrepancy, possibly explained by levirate succession), is the most significant post-exilic Davidic figure in the Chronicler’s genealogy. Historically, Zerubbabel served as the Persian-appointed governor of Yehud (the province of Judah) under Darius I (ca. 522-486 BCE), as attested in the books of Haggai (Haggai 1:1: ‘Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah’) and Zechariah (Zechariah 4:6-10: YHWH’s word to Zerubbabel, ‘not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit’). Haggai addresses Zerubbabel as YHWH’s ḥôtām (signet ring, Haggai 2:23) — a reversal of the curse on Coniah/Jehoiachin (Jeremiah 22:24, where Coniah is torn off as a signet ring). The Chronicler’s extension of the Davidic genealogy six generations beyond Zerubbabel (into figures otherwise unknown: Hananiah, Shelomith, Hashubah, Ohel, Berechiah, Hasadiah, Jushab-hesed in vv19-20; then further in vv21-24) provides a chronological anchor: six generations at 25 years per generation places the Chronicler’s composition ca. 400-350 BCE, in the late Persian period. The genealogy demonstrates that the Davidic line survived exile and continued into the Chronicler’s own era — a live covenant claim, not merely a historical record.
1: David's sons born at Hebron open the royal genealogy. Six sons of six different wives reflect the ANE diplomatic practice of cementing alliances through marriage: Ahinoam of Jezreel (a local Judahite alliance), Abigail the Carmelite (a prestigious match after Nabal's death), Maacah daughter of Talmai king of Geshur (a Transjordanian political alliance), Haggith/Abital/Eglah (less documented alliances or domestic wives). The international scope of David's Hebron marriages mirrors his growing political authority even before Jerusalem.
2: Absalom son of Maacah the Geshurite princess carries the most politically volatile genealogical combination: Davidic father + foreign royal mother. His rebellion against David (2 Samuel 15–18) may reflect the tension of a prince who felt his Geshurite royal blood entitled him to succeed without waiting for David's explicit designation. The Chronicler lists him without narrative comment, preserving the genealogical record of even the most tragic royal son.
3: Adonijah son of Haggith is the fourth son of Hebron; after Amnon's death and Absalom's death, he was the eldest surviving son and attempted to claim the throne before Solomon was crowned (1 Kgs 1). The Chronicler omits this narrative entirely, but the genealogical placement — fourth son, mother unknown beyond her name — subtly conveys why Adonijah believed he had a claim Solomon's supporters had to actively counter.
4: "Six were born to him in Hebron, where he reigned seven years and six months. And he reigned thirty-three years in Jerusalem" — the Chronicler's regnal summary for David (7.5 + 33 = 40.5 years total; cf. 2 Samuel 5:4's round 40 years) grounds the genealogy in chronological history. Forty years of reign, six sons at Hebron, thirteen+ sons at Jerusalem: the multiplication of David's line is the genealogical enactment of the Abrahamic promise to multiply the patriarch's descendants.
5: Shimea, Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon — all sons of Bathshua (Bath-Sheba) daughter of Ammiel — are listed as sons of a single mother. The Chronicler names Bathshua's father "Ammiel" (cf. 2 Samuel 11:3's "Eliam" — the same person, names reversed/varied). Nathan son of David appears in Luke's genealogy of Jesus (Luke 3:31) as the non-Solomonic Davidic line through which the Messiah descended on Mary's side, though this Christological significance was not available to the Chronicler.
6: Ibhar, Elishama, Eliphelet — these Jerusalem-born sons appear in both the Chronicles list and the Samuel parallel (2 Samuel 5:15), though with some name variations. The multiplication of sons at Jerusalem reflects David's full royal establishment: multiple wives, a palace, and a stable kingdom. The Chronicler lists nine names in vv6–8 (some lists differ by 1–2) — the Jerusalem royal household at its fullest.
7: Nogah and Nepheg appear in Chronicles but not in the parallel 2 Sam 5 list, representing either sons omitted from the shorter Samuel account or sons born after the Samuel source was compiled. The Chronicler's longer list may draw on temple archive records that preserved a more complete royal genealogy than the court history preserved in Samuel.
8: Elishama (second listing), Eliada/Beeliada, and Eliphelet (second listing) — textual variants appear here compared to the Samuel parallel. "Beeliada" ("Baal knows") in 2 Samuel 5:16 became "Eliada" ("God knows") in Chronicles — a deliberate substitution consistent with Chronicles' tendency to replace Baal-names with El-names. The Chronicler edits theologically even in genealogies.
9: "All these were David's sons, besides the sons of the concubines" — a summary note that acknowledges David's full household while preserving Tamar's name. Tamar, David's only named daughter in the genealogies, appears here without reference to her violation by Amnon (2 Samuel 13). The Chronicler's silence on that episode contrasts sharply with his inclusion of Tamar as a named individual — she is remembered as a person, not erased because of what was done to her.
10: Solomon → Rehoboam: the Davidic dynasty enters its sequential listing. The seventeen names from Solomon to Jeconiah/Jehoiachin (vv10–16) represent four centuries of Judahite monarchy. The Chronicler's audience knew this list not as ancient history but as their grandparents' world — the last kings before Babylon destroyed Jerusalem were within living memory of the oldest returnees.
11: Rehoboam → Abijah → Asa: three generations spanning the kingdom's division through the beginning of religious reform. Asa's reign marks the first sustained effort at covenant renewal after the apostasy of the divided monarchy era. The Chronicler will devote detailed narrative to Asa's reform (2 Chr 14–16) while the genealogy here notes him only as a link in the chain — the narrative and genealogy together create the full portrait.
12: Asa → Jehoshaphat → Joram: Jehoshaphat's alliance with the Omrides (through his son Joram's marriage to Athaliah, daughter of Ahab) is genealogically embedded here. The genealogy moves past these alliances without comment, but readers who knew the narrative (2 Chr 18–22) would hear the theological resonance: the Davidic dynasty survived even when its kings made catastrophic alliances with Israel's apostate north.
13: Joram → Ahaziah → Joash: Ahaziah's reign lasted one year before Jehu's coup (2 Chronicles 22:2–9). Joash/Jehoash was hidden in the temple for six years while Athaliah (his grandmother) ruled, then crowned at age seven (2 Chr 23). The genealogy compresses six years of temple exile into a single name-link, but the memory of that miraculous preservation — YHWH keeping the Davidic lamp burning during the deepest darkness — resonates powerfully for a community returning from Babylonian exile.
14: Amaziah → Azariah (Uzziah) → Jotham: the mid-monarchy period of relative stability. Uzziah's 52-year reign was the longest in Judah's history; his pride in entering the temple to burn incense (2 Chronicles 26:16–21) resulted in leprosy, which the genealogy entirely passes over. The genealogical format strips away narrative; only the dynastic continuity matters here.
15: Jotham → Ahaz → Hezekiah: the deepening crisis of the Assyrian period. Ahaz's apostasy (2 Chr 28) nearly ended the dynasty; Hezekiah's reform (2 Chr 29–32) rescued it. The single-line genealogical format places Ahaz between two reformers (Jotham and Hezekiah) without explanation — again the narrative supplements what genealogy compresses.
16: Hezekiah → Manasseh → Amon → Josiah: the final century of the Judahite monarchy. Manasseh's 55-year reign as the longest and initially most apostate king (2 Chronicles 33:1–9) ended in repentance after Assyrian captivity (2 Chronicles 33:10–17) — a repentance the Chronicler uniquely records. Josiah's sweeping reform (2 Chr 34–35) gave the monarchy its last great moment of covenant faithfulness before Babylon arrived. Four kings, one century, the arc of collapse and hope.
17: Josiah's sons — Johanan (firstborn, died before reigning), Jehoiakim (second, reigned 11 years under Babylon), Zedekiah/Mattaniah (third, last king, renamed by Nebuchadnezzar), and Shallum/Jehoahaz (fourth, reigned 3 months) — reigned in non-birth-order succession reflecting Egyptian and Babylonian imperial manipulation of Judah's throne. The Chronicler lists them in birth order while their reign order was: Jehoahaz (4th) → Jehoiakim (2nd) → Jehoiachin (Jeconiah's son) → Zedekiah (3rd).
18: Jeconiah/Jehoiachin's captive status is implicit in "the sons of Jeconiah, the captive" — the Hebrew word ʾāsîr (prisoner/captive) becomes his permanent epithet, encoding the Babylonian deportation of 597 BCE into the genealogical record. Yet from this captive king comes Shealtiel and the line that returns from exile. The genealogy insists: captivity does not end dynasties; YHWH's purposes continue through exile.
20: Hashubah, Ohel, Berechiah, Hasadiah, and Jushab-hesed — five more sons of Zerubbabel — complete the first post-exilic generation. The name Jushab-hesed ("lovingkindness/covenant-loyalty has returned") is a theological confession: the child's name declares that YHWH's covenantal faithfulness has returned to Israel after the exile. The Chronicler preserves this name as a living testimony to post-exilic hope.
21: Hananiah → Pelatiah and Jeshaiah; then Rephaiah's descendants; then Arnan's and Obadiah's and Shecaniah's lines. The post-exilic Davidic genealogy extends three to four generations beyond Zerubbabel, suggesting the Chronicler compiled his work sometime in the 5th–4th century BCE when these descendants were still identifiable community members. The continuation of the Davidic line, however obscure, was itself a form of messianic witness.
22: The sons of Shecaniah — Shemaiah and his six sons — provide the genealogy's most recent names, likely the living generation at the time of composition. These men were not kings, not governors, not even mentioned in other texts: they were ordinary members of the Davidic family living in Persian-period Yehud. The Chronicler lists them as carefully as he listed Solomon because the covenant promise had not been revoked — it simply awaited fulfillment.
23: Neariah's three sons and Elioenai's seven sons push the Davidic genealogy to its furthest chronological extent. Elioenai is likely one of the latest-generation names the Chronicler could verify from community records. Seven sons for the last named Davidite mirrors the seven sons of Jesse — a literary symmetry suggesting completeness, or perhaps simply reflecting the actual family size of this branch's head.
24: The seven sons of Elioenai — Hodaviah/Eliashib/Pelaiah/Akkub/Johanan/Delaiah/Anani — close the Davidic genealogy of Chronicles. Anani as the final name has attracted messianic interpretation in later tradition (the Aramaic word for "cloud" appears in Daniel 7:13's "one like a son of man coming with the clouds"), but the Chronicler's purpose is simpler: to show that the Davidic line persisted, generation by generation, from Jesse to the present, keeping alive the covenant between YHWH and his anointed.
